chapters.title: New facts about our mysterious neighbors
chapters.text:
Fungi are quiet, mysterious, but influential companions in almost every aspect of our lives. For more than a billion years, they have been supporting the vital activity of plants, cleaning the environment from pollution, becoming components of medicines and food, and affecting animals and humans. Although their influence is so extensive, they live a secret life, and more than 90% of their species have not been studied yet.

The mushroom kingdom is ancient and remarkable. Without the activity of fungi, there would be no plants, which they helped emerge from the water 500 million years ago; they still support 90% of terrestrial plants by providing them with nutrients.

Fungi exist imperceptibly in almost every place on Earth — on the seabed, in deserts, in the north, and in our bodies. In fact, even the most extreme environments can be suitable for fungi to survive. They reproduce by spores, and many fungi create new ways to thrive, making themselves attractive to humans and animals to carry their "seeds" all over the world.

Human life is also surprisingly intertwined with fungi; we have learned to cooperate with mushrooms. For example, in 1928, Alexander Fleming discovered that a specific mold produced penicillin, which became the first antibiotic and saved many lives. In 2017, researchers rebuilt the diet of Neanderthals and found that an ancient man with a toothache consumed penicillin-producing mold. Perhaps, people realized its medicinal properties long before the official discovery.

Scientists have studied and described only 6% of all types of fungi. And while humans are getting closer to the mysteries of mushrooms, the latter continue to support life on Earth, combining plants into a large-scale ecosystem, the so-called “wood wide web.”

Despite their nearness, fungi are so mystifying, their possibilities so ‘other.’ ~ Merlin Sheldrake

Merlin Sheldrake

Merlin Sheldrake admitted that the mushrooms changed his mind every time he encountered them. The plant and animal kingdoms receive attention from scientists, while fungi play the role of "gray cardinals" — powerful, full of potential, but secretive. In this summary, you will read about the extraordinary abilities and benefits of fungi, learn more about the connections between living things and reconsider your views on the exciting power of wildlife.

The Fungi web, which hides under the soil, silently and attentively supports the existence of flora while remaining in the shade.

chapters.title: The expensive and rare delicacy from the ground and how to find it
chapters.text:
We hardly ever thought of fungi as intelligent beings, and, even for scientists, the study of mushrooms is undefined. However, behind the seemingly inconspicuous shell of fungi lies the ability to solve problems and learn.

Our journey into the world of fungi begins with truffles. Our first association with this type of fungi is their price: two kilograms can cost 12,000 euros, or over $1,300.00! Secondly, truffles' unusual taste and aroma make them a delicacy. The attractiveness of a truffle emerged out of their need to reproduce; the animals that procure the truffle spread its spores and prolong its existence. These fungi’s chemical elements lure animals and tell them that the mushroom is safe to eat. Smell has become part of our interaction with the mushroom; fragrances created by mushrooms have become part of our desire to be attractive. For example, the resin of the Agar tree, which appears as a result of fungal contamination, is used to manufacture perfumes.

The fragrant fruiting bodies of truffles result from mushroom "mating" — the hyphae of one mycelium system merges with the hyphae of another (the mycelium is an artificially created root system of the fungus, and the hyphae are its filaments). Also, If the hyphae do not find a tree partner, they will die. Mycorrhizal trees provide carbon to truffles, and fungi help the tree’s roots absorb nutrients better. In the endless jumble of various organisms in the soil, plants use chemical compounds to find each other.

Sheldrake joined the truffle hunters to watch the process of finding them. He observed the hunters enter the forest dressed in camouflage to avoid attracting the attention of other hunters and accompanied by dogs trained to locate the mushrooms. According to Sheldrake, the dogs even looked like truffles. The hunters had been searching for the cherished mushrooms for a while when one of the dogs jumped off the trail and began to dig the ground; it had found a truffle, and its aroma was unusual and intense.

Mushrooms’ smell and taste create a chemical language to communicate with animals and people.

Did you know? Mushrooms produce about 50 megatons of spores each year, which is the weight of 500,000 whales.

chapters.title: Everything is connected — underground webs prove it
chapters.text:
The mycelium network permeates most of the world with its filaments. If you isolate the mycelium from one gram of soil and arrange all its threads one after another, its length can be tens of kilometers. These mycelium webs live in labyrinths in the soil, have learned to decipher spatial puzzles, and even help scientists solve human problems. For example, researchers at the University of the West of England have leveraged slime molds' pathfinding abilities to assist in developing fire escape plans.

The mycelium grows to explore different directions and chooses the most beneficial to connect it with food. However, to achieve this, one part of the mycelium must be aware of what is occurring in other parts. Swedish mycologist Stefan Olsson tried to decipher the essence of this connection. He chose Panellus stipticus for his experiments — a mushroom that glows in the dark and attracts insects. When he pricked the edge of the mushroom with a pipette, the affected area illuminated; he suggested that the hyphae had thrown some compounds into the air. Then he grew two identical mycelia next to each other and pricked one of them. The injured mushroom system shined but the other did not, so he concluded that each individual mycelium uses specific communication channels to alert of danger or food.

Olsson suggested that mycelium networks transmit signals to each other using electricity. He inserted electrodes into the fungal hyphae and noticed regular impulses similar to the sensory neurons of animals. He then placed a piece of wood, which fed the mushroom, on the mycelium a few centimeters from the electrodes and found that the intensity of the mushroom's pulses doubled.

Mycelium networks can be compared to polyphony — equal voices that function independently to form a melody.

Researcher Andrew Adamatzky’s article, Towards fungal computer, suggested that electrical signals inside mushrooms could become prototypes of biocomputers and could tell people about soil and water quality or environmental pollution. But so far, humanity knows too little about the abilities and intelligent behavior of mushrooms. In 1817, Charles Darwin noted that intelligence is the ability of species to do what is necessary for their survival. The same can be said for fungi: their minds are far from our concept of consciousness, but their ability to survive shows that the concepts of the mind can be expanded.

chapters.title: The story of the brave fungi journey into space and how it will help people
chapters.text:
The desire of humans to learn more about fungi has led to the mushroom's journey into space. In 2016, several species of lichens that had been in space for a year and a half returned to Earth. As part of the BIOMEX project, scientists investigated the possibility of the survival of biological samples in extraterrestrial conditions. The ability of lichens to survive is so impressive that it can push the boundaries of opportunities for other terrestrial life forms. The BIOMEX program has found that some hardy lichen species can restore metabolism after dehydration and heal space-inflicted wounds.

In 1869, botanist Simon Schwendener published an article on the dual nature of lichens. He claimed that they are composed of the essences of a fungus and an alga. In 1877, German botanist Albert Frank introduced the term "symbiosis" to describe this partnership. So, lichens became an example of how the components of the kingdom of nature cooperate — the mushroom component provides physical protection and extracts minerals, and the algal component absorbs light and carbon dioxide for energy.

In 1967, biologist Lynn Margulis put forward the theory that several vital stages of evolution occurred due to the coexistence of different organisms. Later, this idea became known as the endosymbiotic theory and opened a new page in the history of the biological picture of the world.

A partnership can be called symbiosis only when the members of the union are useful to each other.

Lichens exist on 8% of the planet, an area larger than tropical forests, living on stones, trees, and rocks. But their love for stone sometimes causes human inconvenience. In 2019, the residents of Easter Island launched a campaign to clean the monumental stone heads, known as moai, as lichens had started to erode their faces.

The ability of lichens to survive in difficult climatic conditions allows them to be called extremophiles — they can revive even after immersion in liquid nitrogen — but, interestingly, they are not capable of photosynthesis. Still, collaboration with algae has presented new possibilities for lichen’s survival. In space, the lichen learned to fall into suspended animation and could be reanimated even ten years later. Thus, the lichen can possibly open new ways for people to travel in space.

chapters.title: The psilocybin effect is like the Cheshire cat smile: it remains when all else is gone
chapters.text:
Some people consider certain types of fungi as their guides to the unconscious and the Universe. These are psilocybin mushrooms, which have become part of human rituals and spiritual practices. For example, shamans use the red-and-white-spotted mushroom to experience the joy and the appearance of visions.

As a child, Sheldrake met family friend and ethnobotanist Terence McKenna, who is one of the leading researchers in psychedelic mushrooms. He authored The Stoned Ape Hypothesis, which states that human consciousness appeared after using psychedelic mushrooms. In the 1960s, many scientists began to massively research LSD and psilocybin as remedies for a range of mental health problems. Psilocybin became an essential and rapidly growing part of the counterculture — it was studied as a potential medicine and used to connect with nature and expand consciousness. In the 1970s, psilocybin and LSD were banned, but in 2016, a new study of people with depression and anxiety showed a decrease in symptoms of psychological disorders after taking psychedelics. The subjects also noted a feeling of joy and delight.

Fungi are capable of altering the consciousness of animals and use this ability to their advantage. Researchers in 2017 found a fungus that hijacked ants' muscles and nervous systems; since it did not have its own mobile body with a central nervous system, it appropriated one. The fungus Ophiocordyceps affects the brains of ants, causing them to lose their fear of heights. The ants climb and close their jaws around the top of the tall plant under the fungal influence. This fungus then sprouts through the ants’ bodies and rains a stream of spores onto the ants below, thus reproducing.

Why do mushrooms need psilocybin? Sheldrake suggests that its evolutionary value may have come from its ability to mobilize creatures. For example, psilocybin makes people search for it, carry it from place to place, and grow it — this way, we spread the spores of fungi.

Our need to find and distribute fungi makes us part of their evolutionary path.

chapters.title: Silent partners: how the union of plants and fungi changed the world
chapters.text:
Green algae began to creep out onto the earth about 600 million years ago and evolved into plants, possibly due to a relationship with fungi called a mycorrhizal relationship. Almost 90% of all plants depend on mycorrhizal fungi, which have become the basis of nearly the entire plant world. The word "mycorrhiza" was coined by the biologist Albert Frank in 1885 (he is also the author of the word "symbiosis").

Mycorrhizal fungi are so prolific that their mycelium makes up between a third and a half of the living mass of soils. ~ Merlin Sheldrake

Merlin Sheldrake

Plants and fungi lead an entangled life. Plant roots tirelessly probe the soil for fungi to develop a relationship with, and vice versa; fungi seek out sources of nutrients. Fungi can determine where a certain plant will grow and even trigger a new species' emergence. In 2018, scientists published a study suggesting that a tendency toward mycorrhizal bonds may have influenced the migration rate of different tree species. But sometimes, fungi can refuse to help plants. These results are difficult to predict — it all depends on the types of fungi and plants and their habitat.

Phosphorus deficiency is a major factor hindering plant growth. Mycorrhizal fungi can extract it from the soil and transfer it to partner plants, which helps them grow better and bigger. Fungi also supply zinc and copper and provide plants with water. In turn, the plants give the mushrooms 30% of the carbon they extract. Plant development also is affected by their cooperation with fungi. For example, tomatoes become sweeter, while basil gains different flavors.

When you savor the fruit of a plant, you are most likely tasting the result of a mycorrhizal relationship.

The mycelium is a kind of knot that holds the soil. Without it, the soil will erode rapidly—mycorrhizal fungi boost the soil's water absorption capacity by 50%. This symbiosis opens new opportunities for the union members. Much like the interaction of people and horses that led to the emergence of transport, fungi and plants are examples of living organisms' striving to create connections.

chapters.title: Reciprocal exchange: what happens in the world's largest network
chapters.text:
The “wood wide web” is enormous, and processes of mutual help are constantly taking place in it. Experiments at the end of the 20th century showed that carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water are transmitted through fungi networks. All 25,000 species of orchid, one of the most prosperous plant kingdoms, are mycohets (plants that depend on fungi). Sheldrake recalls that he could not grow an orchid spout until he connected it with fungi.

Resources are transported through fungal networks from larger plants to smaller ones. But why would plants transfer resources to a mushroom that will give them to another competing plant? Presumably, they can benefit from such an exchange, but at different times of the year.

The fungi, which become members of the mycorrhizal relationship, have benefits as they can find more sources to feed. Living organisms in mycorrhizal relationships survive better than plants outside the network. The wood wide web not only transports resources but also moves hormones, which regulate growth and development.

Also, the fungi can broadcast a warning about danger through the network. If aphids attack a plant, the fungus will also suffer, so it will amplify the chemical alarm. At the same time, the mushroom will always prefer a healthy plant if there is a choice between plants in danger. Mycelium networks never rest, never stop looking for partners and never stop helping them. Everything that happens inside them is an ongoing process.

Fungal systems are dynamic, and they provide the continuous movement of nutrients in nature.

chapters.title: How mushrooms help our ecology — independently and with the help of people
chapters.text:
Fungi have acquired an outstanding ability to survive environmental disasters; they can adapt to complexities and seek beneficial connections. Sheldrake wondered if people in the future could cooperate with fungi, especially in the case of a problematic climatic situation.

One of the most impressive capabilities of fungi is their ability to break down lignin (a solid and complex polymer found in wood). When trees died millions of years ago, they did not decompose for a long time. Layers of non-decayed forest accumulated, and the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere fell, leading the planet to hypothermia. After a long time of evolution, fungi have learned to break down lignin. Mycologist Peter McCoy, the founder of Radical Mycology, believes fungi can solve different technological and environmental problems. Scientists discovered that oyster mushroom mycelium grows and eats away at used diapers. McCoy also taught oyster mushrooms to digest cigarette butts — the mushrooms were able to overcome the toxic substances of cigarettes.

In the process of evolution, fungi have found different ways to decompose garbage. However, we cannot say for sure that there is a single fungi solution for all environmental problems since their behavior is impossible to predict.

McCoy believes there is a need to discover and test new fungal strains. Scientists were able to find a strain that could process polyurethane plastic due to this approach. The mycelium of some fungi can quickly heal wounds and restore the skin. The American company Ecoactive Design uses mycelium to create building materials. It sends agricultural waste to feed its mushrooms; as a result, strong and light materials grow out of sawdust. The company strives to displace plastic and stop using animal skins. Ecoactive Design is already partnering with IKEA; NASA is also interested in the possibility of collaboration — mycomaterials can be helpful for constructions on the Moon.

The development of fungi technologies is beneficial for humanity and safe for the environment unless future research proves otherwise.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Fungi are closely intertwined in people's lives. Yeast mushrooms live in our bodies, and we have learned to use yeast for many purposes — brewing, winemaking, and bread making. Yeast domesticated people and forced them to lead a sedentary lifestyle, and because of the possibility of making bread and beer, people began to abandon nomadism. Alcohol had a unique effect on human consciousness; the ancient Greeks even worshiped a god of winemaking.

People are still trying to understand fungi and find classifications for them, but their forms, properties, and abilities continue to elude any identification. We still don't really know what mushrooms are. However, the fact that they have a specific mushroom consciousness and life structure is beyond doubt.

Since childhood, Merlin Sheldrake has been interested in fungi, and he realized that the vast majority of processes in the world are impossible without their intervention. Therefore, he grew oyster mushrooms in one of the copies of his book; when they sprouted and destroyed the book, he ate them. From another copy, he took out pages, ground them, made a sugar solution, added yeast, and made beer.

Today you are making mushroom soup at home; it tastes good and gives you a feeling of fullness. At the same time, in uncharted forests, fungi nets connect plants, nourish them and support the soil. Perhaps someday, mushrooms will become closer and more understandable to us and will be able to solve many more problems of humankind. In the meantime, they are just nearby, living their entangled lives.

Try this

Fungi maintain the purity of nature in their own way. You can follow these simple guidelines to reduce environmental pollution:

• Try to reduce your plastic consumption.
• Start sorting trash.
• Try giving a second chance to old clothes that you planned to throw away.
• Find out about local manufacturers who make products from eco-friendly materials.

Read it till the end?
